observations.recommendation: c. The Accounting Office provide complete and correct details of the current and future PPE acquisitions for reference in the computation of depreciation;
observations.reason: No action taken by management.

observations.recommendation: d. Thereafter, ensure that depreciation is provided once the asset is available for use.
observations.reason: No action taken by management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile the differences in the reported financial statement balances and implement meticulous monitoring and review measures to ensure that financial statements are henceforth recorded and presented accurately.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Submit a reconciliation report of the intra-agency receivables and intra-agency payables variance of ₱100,351.32, otherwise, exhaust efforts to reconcile the discrepancy; and
Eliminate the reciprocal accounts during the preparation of the financial statements recognizing assets and liabilities in accordance with Paragraph 7 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to annually conduct a physical count of its PPE and immediately prepare and submit its corresponding RPCPPE.
We also recommended and the Inventory Committee and Accounting Office agreed to reconduct the one-time cleansing of its PPE account balances, reconciling the same with property and accounting records in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006.
4.9	We recommended and Management agreed to stop the practice of charging to the LDRRMF expenses which are not enumerated in the aforesaid circular.

We recommended and the Management agreed to observe the provisions embodied in the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Vehicles.
We also recommended and Management agreed to prepare and properly fill up the forms and reports prescribed in the manual.

We recommended and the Human Resource Management Officer or responsible personnel agreed to submit within five working days from execution/issuance the JOCs and their supporting documents for review by the Auditor.
We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to diligently monitor stale checks and communicate with the payees for their timely encashment.
We also recommend and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary adjusting entries regarding the outstanding checks.
c. Ensure the complete preparation and timely monthly submission of the report on sources and utilization of DRRMF.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct all the Department Heads/employees concerned to submit their detailed action plans on the CY 2022 AAR audit observations and recommendations including that of prior years, related/connected to their departments/offices, and the information gathered therein be used to properly accomplish the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation, and submit the same to the Audit Team.

We also recommended and Management agreed that henceforth, the LGU of San Lorenzo shall, within 60 days from receipt of the Annual Audit Report submit to the COA, either in printed form or by way of electronic document, a status report on the actions taken on said audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to: 
Ensure that programs and projects identified in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan and utilizations of the LDRRMF are in accordance with NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; and 




Ensure the complete preparation and timely monthly submission of the report on sources and utilization of DRRMF.

Management’s Comments
The Municipal Accountant and Treasurer informed the audit team during the exit conference that they had already communicated with the payees of the identified checks and that the payees agreed to update them on what they intended to do with these stale checks so that the Accounting and Treasury Offices could take appropriate action and adjust the books accordingly.
This finding is covered by Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) No. 2024-03 dated April15, 2024.
We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to diligently monitor stale checks and communicate with the payees for their timely encashment.
We also recommend and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary adjusting entries regarding the outstanding checks.

Management’s Comments
The Municipal Accountant provided the following comments on the noted discrepancies:
a. These unusual balances are due for reconciliation.
b. The adjustment for this discrepancy has been made for January 2024 balances.
c.1. The difference in Personal Services represents the refund for overpayment of salaries, which was not reflected in the Statement of Appropriations, Allotments, Obligations, Disbursements, and Balances (SAAOB).
c.2. As per the record of the Budget Office, capital outlay has been reflected as MOOE in the SAAOB but was properly recorded in the presentation of the financial statements.
This finding is covered by AOM No. 2024-02 dated March 1, 2024.
We recommendedand the Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile the differences in the reported financial statement balances and implement meticulous monitoring and review measures to ensure that financial statements are henceforth recorded and presented accurately.

Management’s Comments
During the exit conference, LDRRMO explained that the CY 2023 DRRM plan and budget were finalized in 2022, prior to the CY 2022 audit exit conference, where the issue of the inclusion of non-disaster risk management programs/projects in the Disaster Risk Reduction Management (DRRM) plan and budget was first raised. As a result of the discussion at the aforementioned conference, the DRRM office no longer implemented some of these initially planned PPAs since they were identified as being part of the LGU’s regular operations instead.
This finding is covered by AOM No. 2024-05 dated May 07, 2024.
We recommended and Management agreed to:
a.	Ensure that programs and projects identified in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan and utilizations of the LDRRMF are in accordance with NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1;
b.	Ensure that charges against the LDRRMF comply with R.A. 9184, as also provided for in Item 6.7 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC. 2013-1; and
c.	Ensure the complete preparation and timely monthly submission of the report on sources and utilization of DRRMF.

This finding is covered by AOM No. 2024-07 dated June 5, 2024.
We recommended and Management agreed to observe the provisions embodied in the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Vehicles.
We also recommended and Management agreed to prepare and properly fill up the forms and reports prescribed in the manual.

This finding is covered by AOM No. 2024-01 dated February 1, 2024.
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct all the Department Heads/employees concerned to submit their detailed action plans on the CY 2022 AAR audit observations and recommendations including that of prior years, related/connected to their departments/offices, and the information gathered therein be used to properly accomplish the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation, and submit the same to the Audit Team.
We also recommended and Management agreed that henceforth, the LGU of San Lorenzo shall, within 60 days from receipt of the Annual Audit Report submit to the COA, either in printed form or by way of electronic document, a status report on the actions taken on said audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended and the management agreed to the following:

The Accounting Office immediately provide depreciation expenses for the aforementioned undepreciated PPEs using the estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2003-007;

The Inventory Committee prepare and submit the RPCPPE and henceforth ensure its timely preparation and submission in accordance with the provisions in COA Circular 2020-006; and




The Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee reconcile the PPE balances between the RPCPPE and the FS, and the Municipal Accountant present the correct balances in the FS.

We recommended and Management agreed that payments for supplies and materials, as well as other operating expenses of the Municipality, be made directly to the suppliers in compliance with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and all officials concerned agreed to observe the provisions of Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 by ensuring that proper and complete documents are attached to the Disbursement Voucher before the issuance of the check.  

We also recommended and Management agreed to consider economy and efficiency in the conduct of Municipal activities.

We recommended and the Accounting Office agreed to submit the disbursement vouchers and their corresponding supporting documents to the Auditor within the prescribed timeframe for a timely audit of accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General Services Officer or responsible personnel to submit within five working days from execution/issuance all Purchase Orders and their supporting documents for review by the Auditor.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Local Development Council and concerned offices/departments/officials to ensure the feasibility and timely implementation/completion of 20 percent development projects as programmed.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant draw an adjusting journal entry to record back in the books Road Networks, which was erroneously transferred to the Registry of Public Infrastructures, and provide the corresponding depreciation expenses and impairment losses, if any.

We recommended and Management agreed to: a. Direct the Inventory Committee to prioritize the conduct of the physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006, in order to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts, thus, establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability; b. Require the Inventory Committee to ensure timely preparation and submission of the RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor; and c. Require the Property Officer to maintain complete and updated PCs, and the Municipal Accountant and Property Officer to reconcile the PPELCs and PCs, and effect adjustments on any discrepancies noted.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the Inventory Committee to prioritize the conduct and completion of the physical count of PPEs in accordance with the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, in order to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts, thus, establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability, as well as, to ensure timely submission of the RPCPPE to the Auditor; and
Require the Property Officer to maintain updated Property Cards to account for every PPE receipt and disposition, and to reconcile the same with the subsidiary ledgers maintained by the Accountant.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant draw an adjusting journal entry to record back in the books Road Networks, which was erroneously transferred to the Registry of Public Infrastructures, and provide the corresponding depreciation expenses and impairment losses, if any.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that all Contracts/POs contain complete detailed item specifications pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Local Development Council and concerned offices/departments/officials to:
a.  Hasten the implementation and completion of the remaining 2020 and 2021 non-implemented development projects so that the 20 percent Development Fund is optimally utilized in the attainment of the Municipalitys socio-economic development goals; and
b. Extensively plan the projects that should be included in the Annual Development Plan/Annual Investment Program and ensure that the same are feasible for implementation within the timeline or as programmed.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Development Council to:
a. 	Evaluate the necessity of other COVID-related programs/projects/activities in coordination with the Municipal DRRM Officer, and consider reprogramming the unutilized CY 2020 re-aligned 20% development fund to such other COVID-19 programs/projects; include the same in the Annual Investment Program; and implement within the timeline as programmed; and
b. 	Otherwise, re-program the unutilized fund to other priority development programs/projects, when warranted.

We recommended and Management agreed that the designate Municipal DRRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant submit the required reports to COA and strictly adhere to the provisions on submission of reports under COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exert effort to retrieve the records of PPEs with no details and provide the corresponding depreciation expenses, using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to: a. Verify/ check the amounts in liquidation reports submitted to the Provincial Accountant of fund transfers with discrepancies in the balance recorded in the books of the Province; b. Determine the status of liquidations of unsettled fund transfers reflected in the books of the Province that has been fully liquidated in the books of the Municipality, and if necessary, provide the Provincial Accountant of copies of liquidation documents that have been submitted so that the unrecorded settlement will be taken up in the books of the Province; c. Cause the reconciliation of the inter-agency payable account Due to LGUs of the Municipality with that of the reciprocal receivable account Due from LGUs of the Province, and effect the necessary adjusting entries on the variance noted to reflect the correct balances of said accounts; and d. Conduct a periodic reconciliation of inter-agency reciprocal accounts to ensure accurate balances of payable/receivable accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Local Development Council and concerned offices/departments/officials to: a. Hasten the implementation and completion of the remaining 2020 non-implemented development projects so that the 20% DF is optimally utilized in the attainment of the Municipality’s socio-economic development goals; and b. Extensively plan the projects that should be included in the Annual Development Plan/ Annual Investment Program and ensure that the same are feasible for implementation within the timeline or as programmed.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to: a. Coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in order to secure copies of the related supporting documents such as Project Status Report, Certificate of 100 percent Project Completion and Inspection and Acceptance Report, to be used as bases in the reclassification of completed construction projects under the CIP to the appropriate PPE account; and b. Prepare the necessary journal entries to effect the reclassification in the books, so that the said accounts are correctly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to: a. Require the Municipal Accountant to: i. Take up in the appropriate inventory account the cost of supplies, materials and relief goods procured for distribution, and in the corresponding expense account the subsequent issuance/distribution duly supported by the RIS and RSMI; ii. Record in the books the inventory of welfare goods for distribution in custody of the Municipality based on the inventory report prepared; and iii. Prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Card for each inventory of welfare goods/ relief goods procured by the Municipality. b. Require the Supply/ Property Officer to: i. Coordinate with the LDRRM Officer to prepare and submit an Inventory List/ Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution in custody of the Municipality as of December 31, 2020 in the prescribed format in Annex D; and ii. Prepare and maintain Stock Cards, Requisition and Issue Slip, and Report of Supplies and Materials Issued for welfare goods.

We recommended and Management agreed to: a. Cause the inclusion of a warranty clause in succeeding Contracts/ POs under Emergency Procurement of the Bayanihan Act, to ensure assurance of replacement/ recovery on goods delivered with defects, and/ or do not conform to quality standards and technical specifications of the contract; and b. Ensure that all Contracts/ POs contain complete detailed item specifications pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to comply with the posting requirements in the GPPB Online Portal of all projects under the Bayanihan Act undertaken through Negotiated Procurement – Emergency Cases.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the Inventory Committee to prioritize the conduct of the physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006, in order to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts, thus, establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability;
Require the Inventory Committee to ensure timely preparation and submission of the RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor; and
Require the Property Officer to maintain complete and updated PCs, and the Municipal Accountant and Property Officer to reconcile the PPELCs and  PCs, and effect adjustments on any discrepancies noted.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exert effort to retrieve the records of PPEs with no details and provide the corresponding depreciation expenses, using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Local Development Council and concerned offices/departments/officials to:
Hasten the implementation and completion of the remaining 2020 non-implemented development projects so that the 20% DF is optimally utilized in the attainment of the Municipality’s socio-economic development goals; and
Extensively plan the projects that should be included in the Annual Development Plan/Annual Investment Program and ensure that the same are feasible for implementation within the timeline or as programmed.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in order to secure copies of the related supporting documents such as Project Status Report, Certificate of 100 percent Project Completion and Inspection and Acceptance Report, to be used as bases in the reclassification of  completed construction projects under the CIP to the appropriate PPE account;  and
Prepare the necessary journal entries to effect the reclassification in the books, so that the said accounts are correctly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to:
Take up in the appropriate inventory account the cost of supplies, materials and relief goods procured for distribution, and in the corresponding expense account the subsequent issuance/distribution duly supported by the RIS and RSMI;
Record in the books the inventory of welfare goods for distribution in custody of the Municipality based on the inventory report prepared; and
Prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Card for each inventory of welfare goods/relief goods procured by the Municipality.
Require the Supply/Property Officer to:
Coordinate with the LDRRM Officer to prepare and submit an Inventory List/Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution in custody of the Municipality as of December 31, 2020 in the prescribed format in Annex D; and
Prepare and maintain Stock Cards, Requisition and Issue Slip, and Report of Supplies and Materials Issued for welfare goods.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Cause the inclusion of a warranty clause  in succeeding Contracts/POs under Emergency Procurement of the Bayanihan Act, to ensure assurance of replacement/recovery on goods delivered with defects, and/or do not conform to quality standards and technical specifications of the contract; and
Ensure that all Contracts/POs contain complete detailed item specifications pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to comply with the posting requirements in the GPPB Online Portal of all projects under the Bayanihan Act undertaken through Negotiated Procurement – Emergency Cases.

We recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed to submit copies of the supporting documents such as Project Status Report, Certificate of Project Completion, and Inspection and Acceptance Report of the completed projects to the Municipal Accountant. Further, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to draw the necessary journal entries to transfer the cost of completed infrastructure projects under the CIP account to its appropriate asset accounts.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant has to secure copy of the Certificate of Project Completion to be used as basis for journal entry, thus, still recorded under the Due to Other NGAs account in the trust Fund books.
Finding first covered in 2018 AAR.
Reiterated in Audit Observation No.2.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to prioritize the
preparation of the complete 2019 RPCPPE. The report shall include all physically
counted properties of the Municipality and categorized per PPE type, pursuant to
the above mentioned prescribed guidelines and shall be immediately submitted to
COA.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to coordinate with
the Municipal Engineer to secure copies of the related supporting documents
such as: Project Status Report, Certificate of 100% Project Completion and
Inspection and Acceptance Report, to be used as bases in the drawing of journal
entries to record the reclassification/transfer of completed projects under the CIP
to their appropriate PPE accounts. Necessary journal entries shall then be
effected in the books.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that full liquidation
of funds received be immediately submitted to the grantor and Journal Entry
Vouchers be drawn to record the transfer of PPE from the Trust Fund to the
General Fund books.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
a. regularly provide the Provincial Accountant with data on the Province’s
share in the real property and other tax collections made by the
Municipality and ensure reconciliation of the balances at year-end,
b. for fund transfers, provide the Provincial Accountant with the details of
the recorded balances of fund for those payables recorded in 2016 and
prior years,
c. submit liquidation reports to the Province for fund transfers with
projects/programs already implemented/completed, and
d. verify liquidation reports already submitted to the Province and ensure
recording in the Province’s books by requesting a copy of the Journal
Entry Voucher from the Provincial Accountant.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to immediately
draw the necessary adjusting journal entries to record the correct amount of trust
liabilities under the Due to NGAs account.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to conduct a
verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the Due to NGOs/POs
account under the Trust Fund, pursuant to COA Circular No, 2016-005.
Adjustments or allowance for impairment shall then be provided for those
receivables which settlement/collectability can no longer be ascertained.
Moreover, request for write-off should be undertaken, if warranted, in accordance
with the guidelines and procedures in the write-off of dormant receivable
accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to compute and
record in the books, depreciation expenses of these PPE, using the determined
estimated useful lives and straight-line method.

We recommended that Management send written notifications to the
concerned liable persons and demand immediate settlement of their unsettled
disallowance balances.

We recommended that Management:
a. require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the
above-mentioned on-going development projects;
b. direct the Municipal Development Council and the implementing offices
to ensure that only projects feasible and can be completed within the
year are included in the Annual Development Plan; and
c. ensure that the implementing offices are provided with adequate
manpower and skills in the implementation of the 20% development
projects.

We recommended that the designated Municipal DRRM Officer and the
Municipal Accountant submit the required reports to COA and strictly adhere to
the provisions on submission of reports under COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended and Management agreed to increase the amount of bond of the Disbursing Officer to ensure the maximum coverage of accountability.
We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to prioritize the preparation of the RPCPPE and to immediately submit the 2018 report as prescribed in the above mentioned guidelines.
observations.reason:
Members of the Inventory Committee are coping with their workload concurrent with their regular duties/functions, hence, the completion of the report was delayed and 2018 RPCPPE incomplete.
Finding first covered in 2018 Annual Audit Report (AAR).
Reiterated in Audit Observation No.1.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Petty Cash Custodian fully liquidate the petty cash fund every end of the year as prescribed in the above mentioned guidelines.

We recommended and Management agreed that demand letters be sent to the concerned accountable personnel with unliquidated cash advances and no additional cash advances be granted to them without liquidation of the previous ones. Further, the salaries of the concerned personnel with long-overdue cash advances should be withheld until the same are settled/liquidated.
observations.reason: Minimal amount of P1,238.25 is unliquidated and over 30 days as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to verify the validity of the Accounts Payable outstanding for 2 years or more. Valid claims shall be paid while those found without valid claims against the Municipality shall be reverted to unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 98 of PD 1145.

We recommended and Management agreed to: a. require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the above-mentioned development projects; b. direct the Municipal Development Council and the implementing offices to ensure that only projects feasible and can be completed within the year are included in the Annual Development Plan; and c. ensure that the implementing offices are provided with adequate manpower and skills in the implementation of the 20% DF projects.
observations.reason:
The completion of prior year development projects and voluminous projects handled by the Municipal Engineer’s Office contributed to the delay.
Finding first covered in 2017 Annual Audit Report (AAR).
Reiterated in Audit Observation No.9.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to: a. regularly provide the Provincial Accountant with data on the Province’s share in the real property and other tax collections made by the Municipality and ensure reconciliation of the balances at year-end, b. for fund transfers, provide the Provincial Accountant with the details of the recorded balances of fund for those payables recorded in 2016 and prior years, and c. verify liquidation reports already submitted to the Province to ensure recording in the Province’s books by requesting a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher from the Provincial Accountant and immediately submit liquidation documents for those unliquidated fund transfers recorded by the Province as Due from LGUs.
observations.reason:
Reconciliation between the Province and the Municipality was on-going.
Finding first covered in 2017 AAR.
Reiterated in Audit Observation No.4.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide Allowance for Impairment for Due to NGOs/POs account. Also, request for write-off should be undertaken in accordance with the guidelines and procedures in the write-off of dormant receivable accounts as provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant has to:
> Secure breakdown/list of NGOs
> Verify accounts
> Provide allowance for impairment.
Finding first covered in 2018 AAR.
Reiterated in Audit Observation No.6.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal DRRM Council and the Officer-in-Charge – DRRM Office, agreed to ensure that allocations in the MDRRMFIP are aligned with the approved budget for DRRM Fund. Moreover, the Plan shall include the activities to be funded from the unexpended DRRM Fund of the previous years, transferred to the Special Trust Fund, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the GAD Focal Person agreed to submit a copy of the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report for CY 2018 to the Audit Team. Henceforth, reports for the succeeding years shall be prepared and submitted within the prescribed period in accordance with the guidelines mentioned.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to prioritize the
preparation of the RPCPPE and to immediately submit the 2018 report as
prescribed in the above mentioned guidelines.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
a. regularly provide the Provincial Accountant with data on the
Province’s share in the real property and other tax collections made
by the Municipality and ensure reconciliation of the balances at
year-end,
b. for fund transfers, provide the Provincial Accountant with the details
of the recorded balances of fund for those payables recorded in
2016 and prior years, and
c. verify liquidation reports already submitted to the Province to
ensure recording in the Province’s books by the Provincial
Accountant and immediately submit liquidation documents for those
unliquidated fund transfers recorded by the Province as Due from
LGUs.

We recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed to submit copies
of the supporting documents such as Project Status Report, Certificate of
Project Completion, and Inspection and Acceptance Report of the completed
projects to the Municipal Accountant. Further, we recommended and the
Municipal Accountant agreed to draw the necessary journal entries to transfer
the cost of completed infrastructure projects under the CIP account to its
appropriate asset accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to verify the
validity of the Accounts Payable outstanding for 2 years or more. Valid claims
shall be paid while those found without valid claims against the Municipality
shall be reverted to unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 98 of
PD 1145.

We recommended and Management agreed that demand letters be sent
to the concerned accountable personnel with unliquidated cash advances and
no additional cash advances be granted to them without liquidation of the
previous ones. Further, the salaries of the concerned personnel with long-
overdue cash advances should be withheld until the same are
settled/liquidated.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide
Allowance for Impairment for Due to NGOs/POs account. Also, request for
write-off should be undertaken in accordance with the guidelines and
procedures in the write-off of dormant receivable accounts as provided under
COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and Management agreed to increase the amount of
bond of the Disbursing Officer to ensure the maximum coverage of
accountability.

We recommend that the Petty Cash Custodian fully liquidate the petty
cash fund every end of the year as prescribed in the above mentioned
guidelines.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the
above-mentioned development projects;
b. direct the Municipal Development Council and the implementing
offices to ensure that only projects feasible and can be completed
within the year are included in the Annual Development Plan; and
c. ensure that the implementing offices are provided with adequate
manpower and skills in the implementation of the 20% DF projects.

We recommended and the Municipal DRRM Council and the Officer-in-
Charge – DRRM Office, agreed to ensure that allocations in the MDRRMFIP are
aligned with the approved budget for DRRM Fund. Moreover, the Plan shall
include the activities to be funded from the unexpended DRRM Fund of the
previous years, transferred to the Special Trust Fund, in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the GAD Focal Person agreed to submit a copy
of the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report for CY 2018 to the
Audit Team. Henceforth, reports for the succeeding years shall be prepared
and submitted within the prescribed period in accordance with the guidelines
mentioned.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to:
a. validate all records available by conducting actual physical count per office/department;
b. if warranted, request for additional personnel to assist in the conduct of inventory-taking and preparation of report; and
c. prioritize the preparation of the RPCPPE and immediately submit the 2017 report as prescribed in the above mentioned guidelines.
observations.reason:
•The reporting of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) is only designated to the Municipal Treasurer himself.
•Workload of Committee assigned.
•Lacking summary of report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that:
a. the specific estimated useful life for each PPE be determined based on the LGU’s experience on the life of its PPE; and
b. the depreciation expenses using the determined estimated useful lives be computed and recorded in the books.

We recommended and Management agreed that demand letters be sent to the concerned Accountable Officers (AOs) with unliquidated cash advances and no additional cash advances be granted to them without liquidation of the previous ones. Further, the salaries of the AOs with long-overdue cash advances be withheld until the same are settled/liquidated.
observations.reason: •With remaining unliquidated cash advances of P42,536.85 out of P1,199,416.78 total amount of unliquidated cash advances at December 31, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to require the Accountable Officer to refrain from receiving collections which are in excess of her approved maximum accountability in her fidelity bond or consider increasing the amount of bond of the Accountable Officer to ensure the maximum coverage of accountability in case of loss.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the above mentioned on-going development projects;
b. direct the Municipal Development Council and the implementing offices to ensure that the development projects identified in the ADP are feasible and can be implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation; and
c. require the implementation of the development projects within the time schedule as targeted in the ADP and optimally utilize the 20% Development Fund.
observations.reason: Completion of previous year’s planned development projects in 2018.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to:
a. require the concerned project implementers to promptly implement BUB projects and utilize funds within the timeline set in the MOA; and
b. require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately prepare and submit to SAs liquidation documents of those BUB projects with partial or non-liquidation and refund the excess funds accordingly.
observations.reason:
•Lack of technical personnel
•Delays in the release of tranches
•Balance of not refunded excess funds as of December 31, 2018 is P2,400.03.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the Municipal Engineer to ensure that the projects are implemented in accordance with the approved project implementation schedule and ADM guidelines;
b. the ADM projects’ documentary requirements as required in the DBM-DILG JMC 2017-3 be promptly prepared and completed before the receipt of ADM funds or within the two-month period prescribed in the Circular; and
c. the Municipal Accountant recognize in the books the partially completed project as Construction in Progress – Infrastructure Assets and subsequently reclassify the same to its appropriate Infrastructure Asset account upon completion.
observations.reason: There was a request for suspension of work by the Contractor which was approved by Management due to valid reason.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) through the MDRRM Officer to ensure that the PPAs in the MDRRM Plan are aligned with those of the national council’s pre-disaster preparedness programs and activities and that the 70% allocation of the MDRRMF are utilized only for such authorized PPAs.
observations.reason: Management conducted prioritizatio n of PPAs within the National guidelines (RA 10121) in 2019 budget.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to require the MCPC to:
a. articulate properly the PPAs and their objectives along with expected results so that only programs aligned with those specified in the Circular are planned;
b. ensure that the planned PPAs are implemented within the timeline set; and
c. measure results of implemented PPAs and submit a report thereon to the concerned Offices including COA Audit Team for proper evaluation.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to:
a. validate all records available by conducting actual physical count per
office/department;
b. if warranted, request for additional personnel to assist in the conduct
of inventory-taking and preparation of report; and
c. prioritize the preparation of the RPCPPE and immediately submit the
2017 report as prescribed in the above mentioned guidelines.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that:
a. the specific estimated useful life for each PPE be determined based on
the LGU’s experience on the life of its PPE; and
b. the depreciation expenses using the determined estimated useful lives
be computed and recorded in the books.

We recommended and Management agreed that demand letters be sent to
the concerned Accountable Officers (AOs) with unliquidated cash advances and
no additional cash advances be granted to them without liquidation of the
previous ones. Further, the salaries of the AOs with long-overdue cash advances
be withheld until the same are settled/liquidated.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the Municipal Accountant regularly provide the Provincial Accountant
with data on the Province’s share in the real property and other tax
collections made by the Municipality;
b. the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer remit the valid
current due Province’s share in real property and other taxes which
remained unremitted by the Municipality;
c. the Municipal Accountant trace the details of the fund transfers by
requesting copies of Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) of the Province;
and
d. the Municipal Accountant submit liquidation documents for fund
transfers recorded by the Province as Due from LGUs.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Accountable
Officer to refrain from receiving collections which are in excess of her approved
maximum accountability in her fidelity bond or consider increasing the amount of
bond of the Accountable Officer to ensure the maximum coverage of
accountability in case of loss.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the
above mentioned on-going development projects;
b. direct the Municipal Development Council to ensure that the
development projects identified in the ADP are feasible and can be
implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation; and
c. require the implementation of the development projects within the time
schedule as targeted in the ADP and optimally utilize the 20%
Development Fund.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to:
a. require the concerned project implementers to promptly implement
BUB projects and utilize funds within the timeline set in the MOA; and
b. require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to
immediately prepare and submit to SAs liquidation documents of those
BUB projects with partial or non-liquidation and refund the excess
funds accordingly.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the Municipal Engineer to ensure that the projects are implemented in
accordance with the approved project implementation schedule and
ADM guidelines;
b. the ADM projects’ documentary requirements as required in the DBM-
DILG JMC 2017-3 be promptly prepared and completed before the
receipt of ADM funds or within the two-month period prescribed in the
Circular; and
c. the Municipal Accountant recognize in the books the partially completed
project as Construction in Progress – Infrastructure Assets and
subsequently reclassify the same to its appropriate Infrastructure Asset
account upon completion.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) through the MDRRM
Officer to ensure that the PPAs in the MDRRM Plan are aligned with those of the
national council’s pre-disaster preparedness programs and activities and that the
70% allocation of the MDRRMF are utilized only for such authorized PPAs.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to
require the MCPC to:
a. articulate properly the PPAs and their objectives along with expected
results so that only programs aligned with those specified in the
Circular are planned;
b. ensure that the planned PPAs are implemented within the timeline set;
and
c. measure results of implemented PPAs and submit a report thereon to
the concerned Offices including COA Audit Team for proper evaluation.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed that: a. a physical count of PPE for the remaining Offices be conducted; b. the corresponding RPCPPE of all Offices be categorized per PPE type/classification; and c. subsequently, physical count of all properties of the Municipality be conducted annually and the corresponding RPCPPE be submitted to COA not later than January 31 of the succeeding year.
observations.reason: Report submitted is only partial.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to the following: a. for PPE without records, an Appraisal Committee be created which should prepare a timetable to identify PPE accounts that will be given priority to be appraised within the year and come up with the appraisal values and reports on priority PPE items; and b. subsequently, the Municipal Accountant draw Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) to record provision for depreciation of those appraised PPE.
observations.managementAction: Appraisal of PPE; Journalize depreciation expense

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that JEVs be drawn immediately for the cost of completed projects amounting to P3,211,142.51 to transfer the amount of project costs from the Trust Fund to the General Fund books.
observations.managementAction: JEVs for transfer of Trust Fund PPEs to General Fund will be drawn

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to: a. regularly provide the Provincial Accountant with data on the Province’s share in the real property and other tax collections made by the Municipality; b. remit the valid current due Province’s share in real property and other taxes which remained unremitted by the Municipality; c. trace the details of the fund transfers by requesting copies of Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) of the Province; d. submit liquidation documents for fund transfers recorded by the Province as Due from LGUs; and e. conduct periodic reconciliation with the Provincial Accountant.

We recommended and Management agreed that Item 5 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of PD No. 1445 be strictly implemented. Failure of the Accountable officers to liquidate their cash advances should constitute a valid cause for the withholding of their salary and for instituting other sanctions. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that a certification be written on the face of the disbursement vouchers of cash advances that the employee has no previous unliquidated cash advance.
observations.managementAction: Suspension of salaries of accountable persons with past due accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that collectors be required to prepare the RCD at the close of each business day and effect remittance intact daily.

We recommended and Management agreed that the implementation of the seven unimplemented BUB projects be fast tracked and the requirements of their corresponding MOA be immediately complied. Subsequently, compliance with the requirements provided in the MOA for all BUB projects should be within the period prescribed and their implementation should be closely monitored.
observations.managementAction: BUB projects were implemented and liquidated in 2017.
observations.reason: Only P58,216.75 of the total P3,656,352.70 represents on-going projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the BAC agreed to ensure, during preliminary examination of bids, that bid tenders submitted by the bidders will include, among others, brand names of goods in the Technical Specifications. Also, upon award, the same information shall be embodied in the Contract Agreement/Purchase Order between the Municipality and the winning bidder, as prescribed in COA Circular No. 96-010. Moreover, bid evaluation shall be in accordance with the procedures prescribed in Section 30 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The BAC ensures that the brand names and technical specifications of specific items would be reflected in their submitted bid tenders based on their quotation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Supply Officer agreed to furnish the Office of the Auditor a copy of every AIR within 24 hours from acceptance and inspection of the procured items in consonance with Section 6.09 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: Copies of AIR forwarded to COA on a weekly basis.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Development Council through the Municipal Planning and Development Officer agreed to ensure that: a. the utilization of the 20% Development Fund be optimally utilized; b. projects identified in the plan are feasible and with complete documentation; and c. projects are properly scheduled and can be implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation.
observations.managementAction: Issued MDC Execom Resolution No. 01 and No. 05, series of 2017 and SB Resolution No. 91 and No. 98, series of 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary correcting entry in the Trust Fund books to record the transfer of the unspent DRRM fund balance amounting to P589,445.22 from the General to Trust Fund, in accordance with the above regulations.

We recommended and the MDRRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure that only expenditures related to disaster risk reduction and management activities are charged against the 5% MDRRMF, as provided in JMC Circular No. 2013-1.
observations.managementAction: Only expenditure related to disaster and risk reduction will be charged to the fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to observe the disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements the information and details of the unexpended DRRMF in accordance with the reporting guidelines set in COA Circular Nos. 2012-002 and 2014-002.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide depreciation to all depreciable assets in accordance with Section 4 of MNGAS, Volume I and COA Circular No. 2003-007.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant commented that the office is still in the process of retrieving and reviewing the documents supporting the acquisition/improvement/repair costs of the PPE. Depreciation expense for the assets will then be provided accordingly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to: Effect immediately the remittance to the Province of Guimaras of its share in the real property and other tax collections made by the Municipality; Trace the details of the fund transfers and submit liquidation to the Province of Guimaras; and Conduct periodic reconciliation with the Provincial Accountant.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant disclosed that the continued non-reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts was due to the failure of the Provincial Accountant to provide the municipality with data on the Province’s receivable from the Municipality and the latter’s payable to the former. It was also learned that a meeting was held among the Provincial Accountant and the Municipal Accountants to settle the differences in the records, however, the balances as of year-end still did not reconcile.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed that:


        a.       a physical count of  PPE  for  the  remaining  Offices  be
             conducted;


        b. the corresponding RPCPPE of all Offices be categorized  per  PPE
             type/classification; and


        c.       subsequently, physical count  of  all  properties  of  the
             Municipality be conducted annually and the corresponding RPCPPE
             be submitted to COA not later than January 31 of the succeeding
             year.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:


        a. for PPE without records, an Appraisal Committee be created which
           should prepare a timetable to identify PPE accounts that will be
           given priority to be appraised within the year and come up  with
           the appraisal values and reports on priority PPE items; and


        b.  subsequently,  the  Municipal  Accountant  draw  Journal  Entry
           Vouchers (JEV) to record provision  for  depreciation  of  those
           appraised PPE.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant  agreed  that  JEVs  be
   drawn immediately  for  the  cost  of  completed  projects  amounting  to
   P3,211,142.51 to transfer the amount of project costs from the Trust Fund
   to the General Fund books.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:


        a. regularly provide the Provincial Accountant  with  data  on  the
           Province’s share in the real property and other tax  collections
           made by the Municipality;


        b. remit the valid current due Province’s share  in  real  property
           and other taxes which remained unremitted by the Municipality;

        c. trace the details of the fund transfers by requesting copies  of
           Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) of the Province;


        d. submit liquidation documents for fund transfers recorded by  the
           Province as Due from LGUs; and


        e. conduct periodic reconciliation with the Provincial Accountant.

We recommended and Management agreed that Item 5  of  COA  Circular
   No. 97-002 and Section 89 of PD No. 1445 be strictly implemented. Failure
   of the Accountable officers  to  liquidate  their  cash  advances  should
   constitute a valid cause for the withholding  of  their  salary  and  for
   instituting other sanctions.


        We also recommended and the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  that  a
   certification be written on the face of the disbursement vouchers of cash
   advances that the employee has no previous unliquidated cash advance.

We recommended and Management agreed that collectors be required to
   prepare the RCD at the close of each business day and  effect  remittance
   intact daily.

We recommended and Management agreed that the implementation of the
   seven unimplemented BUB projects be fast tracked and the requirements  of
   their corresponding MOA be immediately complied. Subsequently, compliance
   with the requirements provided in the MOA for all BUB projects should  be
   within the period prescribed and their implementation should  be  closely
   monitored.

We recommended and the BAC agreed  to  ensure,  during  preliminary
   examination of bids, that bid  tenders  submitted  by  the  bidders  will
   include,  among  others,  brand  names  of   goods   in   the   Technical
   Specifications. Also, upon award, the same information shall be  embodied
   in the Contract of Agreement/Purchase Order between the Municipality  and
   the winning bidder, as prescribed in COA Circular No.  96-010.  Moreover,
   bid evaluation shall be in accordance with the procedures  prescribed  in
   Section 30 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended and the Supply Officer agreed to furnish the  Office
   of the Auditor a copy of every AIR within 24 hours  from  acceptance  and
   inspection of the procured items in consonance with Section 6.09  of  COA
   Circular No. 95-006 and COA Circular 92-386.

We recommended and the Municipal Development  Council  through  the
   Municipal Planning and Development Officer agreed to ensure that:


         a. the  utilization  of  the  20%  Development  Fund  be  optimally
            utilized,


         b. projects identified in the plan are feasible and  with  complete
            documentation, and


         c. projects are properly scheduled and can  be  implemented  within
            the year with the proposed budget allocation.

We recommended and the Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  make  the
   necessary correcting entry in the Trust Fund books to record the transfer
   of the unspent DRRM  fund  balance  amounting  to  P589,445.22  from  the
   General to Trust Fund, in accordance with the above regulations.

We recommended and the MDRRM Officer and the  Municipal  Accountant
   agreed  to  ensure  that  only  expenditures  related  to  disaster  risk
   reduction and management activities are charged against the 5% MDRRMF, as
   provided in JMC Circular No. 2013-1.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to  observe  the
   disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements the  information  and
   details  of  the  unexpended  DRRMF  in  accordance  with  the  reporting
   guidelines set in COA Circular Nos. 2012-002 and 2014-002.

We recommended and the Head of the Inventory Committee agreed to expedite the physical count of all the PPEs owned by the Municipality, prepare the corresponding RPCPPE and submit the same not later than January 31 of the succeeding year.
observations.reason: The new Municipal Treasurer commented that the Committee was reconstituted on March 16, 2016 only and annual physical inventory shall be undertaken in CY 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to continuously monitor the cash advances and send demand letters to the accountable officers as soon as the purpose, for which the cash advances were granted has been served, in accordance with the provisions of Sections 5.7 and 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. No additional cash advance be granted to the officials/employees pending liquidation or accounting of previous ones.
observations.reason: Notices/reminders were already sent to the concerned employees to demand liquidation of the remaining unliquidated cash advances and their salaries withheld until settlement thereof.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that a vehicle be made available to the ICO-Municipal Treasurer for his bank transactions to ensure the daily and intact deposit of collections pursuant to Section 32, Volume I, of MNGAS for LGUs, to avoid keeping substantial cash in his possession and to safeguard the funds of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to demand liquidation reports from the barangays and Liga ng mga Barangay for the financial assistance granted, and ensure that fund transfers be supported with MOA and other pertinent documents required in COA Circular No. 2012-001. It is further recommended that Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) be drawn to correct the entries made to record the previous releases of fund transfers.
We recommended and the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant agreed to strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth in RA 7305 on the grant of hazard pay to the health employees.

We recommended and the designated Supply Officer agreed to furnish the Office of the Auditor a copy of every AIR within twenty four (24) hours from acceptance and inspection of the procured items in consonance with Section 6.09 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and COA Circular 92-386.
observations.reason: Delayed submission of AIRs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed that all projects identified under the 20% DF be fully implemented/ completed during the year for the constituents to avail of their maximum benefits and utilization.
observations.reason: Delayed implementation of projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed that only the wages of personnel who are directly involved in the disaster-related PPAs be charged against the MDRRMF. Immediate employment of additional rescuers be engaged only in the event of actual disasters and calamities.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to transfer the unexpended QRF and Mitigation Fund-MOOE balance to Trust Fund pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. 10121 and COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended and the GAD Focal Person agreed to establish and maintain GAD Database for storage of necessary information in aid of effective gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation.
